questions.question: Explain the purpose of the 'static' keyword when applied to a variable in a Java class. How does a static variable differ from an instance variable in terms of memory allocation and access?
keyPoints: A static variable belongs to the class itself, not to any individual object instance.
Only one copy of a static variable exists in memory, shared by all instances of the class.
Static variables are accessed using the class name (e.g., ClassName.variableName) rather than an object reference.

questions.question: Describe the key characteristics and rules for a static method. What are the limitations of a static method regarding access to instance members, and why do these limitations exist?
keyPoints: Static methods belong to the class and can be called without creating an instance of the class.
Static methods can only directly access other static members (variables and methods) of the class.
They cannot use the 'this' or 'super' keywords as there is no specific instance context.
The limitation exists because static methods are not tied to an object instance and therefore cannot access instance-specific data which only exists after an object is created.

questions.question: Compare and contrast static and non-static contexts in Java. Discuss the concept of a 'static block', explain its purpose, and detail the complete order of initialization for static variables and blocks when a class is loaded by the JVM.
keyPoints: A static context (methods/blocks) is associated with the class, while a non-static context is associated with object instances.
A static block is used for initializing static variables and executes only once when the class is loaded into memory.

The order of initialization is: static variables are set to default values, then static initializer blocks are executed and static variables are explicitly initialized in the order they appear in the source code.
This initialization occurs when the class is first referenced (e.g., by creating an instance, accessing a static member, or using Class.forName()).
This process is fundamental to understanding class loading and is separate from object instantiation and initialization.
